data.title: Recursion Pharmaceuticals May Be Losing Ground To Its Peers
data.description:
Recursion Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (RXRX) describes itself as a "clinical-stage TechBio company decoding biology to radically improve lives." The company’s vision is based on the premise that most diseases – they estimate around 80% – still lack disease-modifying therapies, and that AI can be harnessed in the drug discovery phase to expedite the process of identifying potential therapies.

It operates through two overlapping drivers: its own investigational pipeline of therapeutic medicines in the areas of oncology, rare disease, neuroscience, and immunology. This program is enabled by what it calls “Recursion OS,” an

AI-native, end-to-end drug discovery and development platform integrating biology, chemistry, and clinical development into a unified intelligence system.

Powered by AI, Recursion OS is intended to quicken the process of drug discovery, and it can be licensed to other biotech companies (partners) as a second potential revenue stream.

The former has not generated any meaningful revenue as the company is still a couple years away at least from approval and commercialization of their pipeline treatments, but the latter has generated several small-ish milestone payments from Sanofi (SNY) to date, alongside other cash inflows ($134 million) from upfront payments. They have a partnership also with Roche/Genentech (RHHBY) which has yielded $213 million in cash inflows to date. They also have collaborations with Bayer (BAYRY) and Merck (MRK), but these have not yet yielded much progress beyond initial trial design and have not yet generated any milestone payments for Recursion.

In total, they have generated over $500 million in upfront and milestone-based payments from their two main partnerships over the last few years.

The company has great potential as a new-age TechBio company that leverages AI to accelerate the process of drug discovery, with its own proprietary pipeline and a licensable operating system to assist partners. However, after initial excitement in the company when it first IPO’d (at $18 a share) in April 2021, close to the height of the Covid bubble in stock prices, it seems investors has lost faith in their ability to deliver meaningful revenue. Or, more likely, investor patience has worn thin due to the path toward meaningful revenue generation being pushed out due to slow progress.

The promises of cutting-edge technology merely hint at a TAM, but revenue growth must follow for investors in these high-risk plays to be duly rewarded. This hasn’t happened fast enough. Indeed, Recursion has seen its stock price plummet 91.5% from its peak in late 2021. Its chart looks ugly - unless you’ve been selling shares short.

In my first coverage of the company nearly six months ago, I acknowledged its potential to disrupt the industry with AI. It had developed strong partnerships and had a robust balance sheet. However, they faced stiff competition from peers operating in the same industry, alongside ongoing operating losses and significant share dilution. They were 18-24 months from meaningful revenue from commercializing their own pipeline, pending drug approvals, and their licensable platform, Recursion OS, had generated only small milestone payments. In a fairly nuanced conclusion to my article, I offered the following thesis:

Given the likelihood of further share dilution and no meaningful revenue until 2027, I suspect shares will continue to fall. I do believe that this company can become profitable, but between now and then I would be very surprised if a more favorable entry point did not present itself. Without trying to sound either noncommittal or just plain ridiculous, I will rate this a short-term Sell, a medium-term Hold, but a Buy for very patient investors.

It seems the first part of my thesis has played out as expected, as shares have fallen 15.6% since then, versus a gain of 8% for the S&P 500 (SP500). Is the company now worthy of an upgrade? I’ll assess recent news and developments in relation to their pipeline and partnerships, and consider various outcomes for when the company reports earnings on May 5.

When discussing the different value propositions for the two sides of their business, the CFO Ben Taylor remarked at the recent 25th Annual Needham Virtual Healthcare Conference:

If you look forward, the partnership business can be more of a steady rolling business than the biotech, which is very up and down based on data – the internal pipeline part of it – but they have different value propositions. If you look at the biotech industry as a whole, good proof-of-concept clinical data can be absolutely transformative, and it is much harder to do that with parts of the partnership business. So, we want both. We want to be able to build our platform and also bring in cash inflows through the partnership business.

So, it seems they view the pipeline business as the one with the strongest growth potential for each indication, but consider the partnership side of the business the one that may come to drive reliable, perhaps even recurring, revenue in the future. The latter would feed the former. That seems to be the goal at this present time.

Internal Pipeline

Recursion has continued to advance its pipeline, showing the latest graphic below on its website. The company has handily differentiated the four Phase 1/2 (human/clinical) trials by showing how some are closer to data read-outs than others. This is helpful, but what does it illustrate about the company’s progress in advancing its pipeline?

I showed a similar graphic back in my November write-up, and what strikes me when comparing the two is that 4 of the 6 trials seem to have not progressed at all. The lines are in exactly the same place as they were almost six months ago. The only trials where real progress seems to be happening is in REC-1245 and REC-4881, alongside REC-4539 which has been un-paused after a hiatus and is now back on track. In their latest presentations, management focused much of their attention on REC-4881, so it makes sense for me to do the same considering this is the most advanced drug in their trial phase and, if approved, the first wholly-owned drug to generate revenue for the company.

Recursion management view REC-4881 as validation of their operating platform, providing "proof of concept" in their technology. Their CEO Najat Khan remarked in their investor presentation:

FAP is a progressive, lifelong disease for which there are currently no approved pharmacotherapies. Nearly 100% of patients end up with colorectal cancer, so the standard treatment is a colectomy (removal of the colon) by the age of 25. In other cases, surgeons might perform a total proctocolectomy (removal of colon and rectum) and perform an Ileal Pouch-Anal Anastomosis, which is to create an internal pouch from the small intestine, to enable normal bowel movements. In any event, a treatment for the disease that allows a patient to avoid this outcome would be a fantastic achievement, and one for which Recursion, should they be successful, will find materially rewarding. FAP is a rare disease, affecting approximately 1 in 8,000 people.

REC-4881, which showed a median 43% reduction in polyp burden in a study read-out in December 2025, has been granted Fast Track and Orphan Drug designations by the FDA.

Recursion aim to engage with the FDA this year with a view to initiating phase 3 trials. While this sounds very promising, let’s consider the risks.

Pipeline Risks

Based on how long the whole process of trials, approval and commercialization takes, approval is unlikely before 2028, and investors should be prepared to wait. Indeed, think about how long it takes to secure ethics approvals, recruit subjects, procure materials, develop protocols, test patients, report results, undertake follow-up analysis, develop and design follow-on studies, secure yet more approvals, recruit still more participants, analyze yet more results, etc.

What this snails-pace progress has proven, if anything, is just how much of a challenge this is and will continue to be for Recursion. Moreover, the Phase 3 trial is the most rigorous and important phase, after they have assessed safety and delivered proof of concept in terms of treatment efficacy. They do not have the resources or experience of a large biotech player.

In the best-case scenario, if they meet both primary and secondary objectives for their Phase 1/2 and also their subsequent Phase 3 trials, they will have to advance the drug through the approval phase, which could take another 6-12 months – even with fast track designation – before revenue would ramp. The company will have to hire a sales team and engage in marketing activities. Overhead costs will balloon, and they are still likely 18-24 months away from securing any revenue from their pipeline. I am possibly being too aggressive here. More realistically, it could be 24-36 months from now before meaningful revenue, if all goes to plan.

Now consider the worst-case scenario. If one of their lead trials, say REC-4881, were not to meet both their primary and secondary objectives in either the Phase 1/2 or 3 trials – proving safe, well tolerated, and effective against placebo – than this could be disastrous for the company and a terrible outcome for shareholders. Of course, this is the case with any early-stage biotech company – they are high risk/high reward plays – but so much more seems to be baked into the company’s progress narrative given the hype around its technology and potential that has developed over the years. Indeed, the share price action though would suggest that some of the gloss has come off the company due to their slow progress and sustained erosion of shareholder value.

Recursion OS

While revenue from their own pipeline is still at least 18 months away if not substantially longer, management anticipate that Recursion OS, their AI-enabled platform to accelerate drug discovery and clinical development, can start generating meaningful revenue through licensing deals. This has already begun through their partnerships.

Recursion OS helps to industrialize the drug discovery process, whereas the previous process was more based around a one-off, trial and error process for each program. The platform integrates AI/machine learning and computation with automated wet labs and large-scale data generation. This means drugs can be discovered, essentially, without human intervention, as robotics and AI undertake and monitor experiments and create data for analysis. This data forms part of a feedback loop, where the data generated from these experiments helps AI models learn, to then enhance and develop future experiments.

The promise of Recursion OS – and other systems like it – is to significantly enhance the scale and speed of drug discovery, as the learnings from experiments and data collection then allow the system to pinpoint and then target areas with the greatest potential. It can, at least in theory, become the engine for driving best-in-class drug discovery. Of course, it has a lot to prove, not just in terms of its effectiveness but also in terms of the business model itself. The key risk for the company is that they are not alone in this race to enable AI for drug discovery. Competition is intense.

Recursion OS Platform Risks: The Competitive Environment

Recursion are not the only company trying to carve out a niche in this exciting new space. In my last article, I outlined their six main competitors:

  • InSilico Medicine
  • Generate BioSciences
  • Absci Corporation
  • Exscientia (acquired by Recursion in November 2024 for $688 million)
  • Isomorphic Labs
  • Owkin.

I considered how much funding they have acquired, their corporate backers, partnerships in tech, biopharma partnerships, and techbio specialization. In this regard, Recursion did not stand out. While Recursion boast support from the Gates Foundation and tech partnerships with Nvidia (NVDA), TempusAI (TEM), Helix, and Google (GOOG), their competitors also have established similarly impressive partnerships. Since my previous article, I can find no new partnerships that Recursion has made – in fact Nvidia sold out its entire equity position by the end of 2025 – yet I note several new developments from their peers.

InSilico Medicine

InSilico Medicine seem to have a similar business model to Recursion. They have their own proprietary pipeline, but harness their “Pharma.AI” platform to accelerate drug discovery. They are currently focused on the areas of "fibrosis, oncology, immunology, pain and obesity and metabolic disorders," with their most advanced program, Rentosertib (formerly known as ISM001-055), considered "a potentially first-in-class drug candidate for the treatment of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis." They are competing in similar areas to Recursion, esp. oncology and immunology. They have technology partnerships with Nvidia, Google Deepmind, and AWS (AMZN).

InSilico Medicine IPO’d on the Hong Kong stock exchange at the end of 2025, and has developed extensive partnerships, signing deals with Eli Lilly (LLY), Sanofi, Fosun Pharma (SFOSF), Exelixis (EXEL), Menarini, Servier, Hygtia Therapeutics, TaiGen Biotechnology among others. Their pipeline is much more impressive than Recursion’s – with ten programs at the clinical phase, and 13 more at the pre-clinical phase – and they are better capitalized, with nearly $450 million in cash and equivalents, giving them a multi-year cash runway. They generated $56 million in revenue in 2025 versus $78 million for Recursion.

Generate Biomedicines

Generate Biomedicines (GENB) is another company in this space that has made progress since my first coverage. Within their own pipeline, they have advanced their severe asthma program targeting TSLP to phase 3 trial. The GB-0895 is AI-engineered, and CEO Mike Nally outlined of the drug and the potential of their Generate Platform:

It demonstrates the potential of programmable biology to design optimal molecular solutions for patients with unprecedented speed and intentionality — in this instance, showing how an antibody engineered with AI can achieve a potentially best-in-class profile and advance into Phase 3 studies within just four years.

They have ongoing research collaboration agreements with Amgen (AMGN) and Novartis (NVS) that are confidential in nature, and a technology collaboration with Nvidia. Their market cap is only slightly smaller than Recursion’s ($1.68 billion versus $1.84 billion), and they generated $32 million in revenue in 2025. They burned through over $200 million in cash last year and have just over $220 million cash and equivalents remaining on their balance sheet. They will certainly have to do a capital raise of some kind this year, but seem much closer to revenue generation from their own pipeline than Recursion. They anticipate the pivotal phase-3 trials for their severe asthma drug (Solairia 1 & 2 trials) to yield results sometime in 2028.

Absci Corporation

Absci Corporation (ABSI) has created a "data-first generative AI drug creation" platform (Origin-1) to harness AI for drug discovery. This is similar to Recursion and their competitors, but they have highlighted as their goal to target "zero-prior" epitopes, that is "designing antibodies for targets and epitopes lacking solved complex structures with protein binders." Novel drug discoveries offer enormous potential for the company, not only through validation but also in terms of generating revenue.

As seen above, Absci also have as their main aim to “industrialize” drug discovery through their AI platform, integrating automated wet labs, data generation, and ongoing model training to create many “shots on goal,” raising their chances of a successful drug candidate at an empirical level.

To date, they have advanced three of their programs to clinical trials, as seen below, but are somewhat behind Recursion in terms of meaningful revenue generation.

They have ongoing research collaborations with Merck, PrecisionLife, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Twist Bioscience (TWST) and Owkin, alongside technology partnerships with Oracle (ORCL) and AMD (AMD). From their latest corporate presentation, they noted that they have received more than $600 million in funding since inception – approximately $100 million more than Recursion – which suggests they have continued to secure funding after my article in November when I reported the amount of $567 million. They remain well funded with $144 million in cash and equivalents, but burned through $94 million last year, generating just $2.8 million in revenue. They remain one to look out for, but have much to prove.

Isomorphic Labs

I highlighted this company in my last coverage as a major threat, given their strong corporate backing – Google is their parent company – talented team/executive board, and partnerships with established biopharma players, Eli Lilly and Novartis. Since then, they have also established another partnership with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ).

They have developed a drug design engine incorporating AI called “Alpha-Fold,” which tries to predict how molecules and proteins will interact before actually testing them. This is so that scientists can save time by testing fewer ideas in the lab, to enable them to target only the most promising candidates, and develop drugs and treatments quicker. By leveraging this platform, the idea is that their customers like Eli Lilly, Novartis, and Johnson & Johnson can save time and money, and deliver drugs to the market quicker.

Recently, they announced the Isomorphic Labs Drug Design Engine (IsoDDE),

a unified computational drug-design system, progressing beyond AlphaFold 3 (AF3) in its predictive accuracy and introducing new capabilities which bridge the gap between structure prediction and real-world drug discovery.

According to research, it more than doubles the accuracy of their original platform, AF3, in terms of protein structure prediction.

It is evident therefore that progress is being made. They have developed a platform that outperforms their first iteration, and this promises to be even more attractive for the large biotech players investing in this technology. Given it is not publicly-traded as a stand-alone entity, financial details are lacking. I note that they received $600 million in an earlier funding round in March 2025, so are likely well-funded for the foreseeable future.

Owkin

Owkin, a France-based privately-held TechBio company, has developed K Pro, an "AI agent to drive insight generation and decision making across drug discovery and development." This is part of their system to develop BASI (biological artificial super intelligence) that promises to use real patient data to expedite drug discovery.

Their business model does not at this point include their own clinical research pipeline, which means it meaningfully differs from Recursion, InSilico, Generate Biomedicines and Absci, but rather – a bit like Tempus AI but on a smaller scale – has created a tech layer for biotech companies to harness. They have partnerships with Sanofi, Merck, Astra Zeneca (AZN) and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) among others, and have generated over $300 million in funding support, making it one of Europe’s leading – or at least better funded – AI drug discovery platforms.

Other Competitors in This Space

If I was to bring this list of competitors up to date, I’d also add Schrödinger (SDGR) and Relay Therapeutics (RLAY) into the mix.

Schrödinger

Schrödinger, like Recursion and some of the others, have both a pipeline and an AI-enabled drug discovery platform. Their pipeline includes several programs, but only SGR-1505 and SGR-3515 (in immunology/oncology) have advanced to clinical trials.

This means they are likely several years away from approval and revenue generation, but investors will be keen to consider the potential of their platform which, like some of the others, has the same anticipated goals. It aims to transform the standard trial-and-error system for drug discovery by incorporating in silico modelling and learning (meaning: using a computer). The platform combines AI and machine learning with its physics-based simulations to generate stronger predictive ability and accuracy.

They license their platform to the life sciences and material sciences industries and use it to develop their own drugs.

They made $256 million in revenue in 2025 and positive cash flow for the first time ($12 million). While they generated $200 million in revenue from their software, the biggest growth driver was in drug discovery, which grew over 100% from 2024 to $56 million in revenue. They are well capitalized with over $400 million in cash and equivalents.

Relay Therapeutics

Relay operates similarly to some of the others. It has developed its own proprietary pipeline of small molecule treatments, mainly in oncology and genetic diseases, generated through utilizing its own AI platform called Dynamo.

This is their own proprietary AI platform for drug discovery, which by 2024 had already developed several novel treatments, notably Zovegalisib, a first-in-class, oral, allosteric, mutant-selective PI3K inhibitor for treating PIK3CA-mutant cancers. Recent study data highlighted strong efficacy. It is not yet approved for use, but clearly investors have seen sufficient potential in the drug and, perhaps more importantly, the platform that helped identify it. Their pipeline has several candidates developed from their Dynamo platform, with Zovegalisib their lead one.

Likely due in part to the success of this drug in triple combination, recent share price performance has been very strong. While it look time for the investor community to come around to the company, their now proven model has helped propel the stock up 366% in the last twelve months.

Relay generated only $15 million in 2025 and burned through $236 million. They have cash and equivalents valued at just under $555 million. This gives them a two-year cash runway but with approval pending in 2028, they may still have to raise cash to cover their costs. Shareholders have also endured significant share dilution over the years as the company have tried to bring their products/treatments to market. The year 2027 should be transformational for the company as they seek approval, thereby fully validating their tech platform.

Taking all of this together, when assessing Recursion’s competitors – and my analysis above is by no means exhaustive – one thing is clear to me, and that is that Recursion does not seem to have a competitive edge in this industry, or at least they have not proven this to be the case. In time, they might. It is also likely there may be some consolidation in this industry, as the large biopharma players will come to decide – through their investments – which of these AI-enabled platform companies will survive and prosper. At this stage, Recursion are not leading the race so have no first-mover advantage, nor have they proven their model superior to Pharma.AI, Generate, Origin-1, Alpha Fold/IsoDDE, K Pro, Schrodinger’s platform, or Relay’s Dynamo. They all seem to do the same thing: harnessing AI to expedite drug discovery.

I am happy to be proven wrong, as there will be Seeking Alpha readers who know far more about the AI technology than I, but on the surface at least it seems that a winner, or two/three, is still to be declared. When assessing the risks of competition in the latest annual report, management highlighted the crowded landscape, but stated:

To respond to these claims, I remain unconvinced that they have proven that Recursion OS, compared to their competitors’ platforms, is more accurate, integrates better with experimental and computational capabilities, or transitions research into clinical development better than its peers. Also, they do not seem to have raised more capital than their peers. Indeed, from my own research, it seems they have raised just over $500 million, which is actually less than most of their peers—all but Owkin. Finally, they have yet to prove their platform, pipeline and business is more scalable than their peers. They have much to prove, yet they currently have a market cap above all but InSilico and Relay:

Since my previous coverage, all of the competitive risks remain. Recursion has yet to take meaningful steps to mitigate these or prove they have a durable competitive advantage in the industry.

Let’s consider their financial data and valuation metrics to assess overall risk/reward with this investment.

Financial and Valuation Considerations

In terms of their financials, the first thing that potential investors should ask is: Does Recursion have sufficient cash or likely FCF generation to last another 2 or 3 years or will they have to do another capital raise and dilute shareholders still further? Indeed, looking at their outstanding share count, it is perhaps easy to see why shareholders have been punished. Dilution has killed shareholder value.

Recursion has an accumulated deficit of just over $2 billion, and this is showing no signs of reversing, as seen below.

Shareholders equity (book value) has increased meaningfully over the years but only because the company has raised cash by issuing more shares, and huge amounts of them. While they have managed to keep debt very low (just $78 million in total), they have issued $533 million in common stock in 2025 alone, on top of the $308.6 million issued in 2024. They also burned through $378 million last year. With cash and equivalents of $743.3 million, this gives them – at the current pace of cash burn – around 7 or 8 quarters of cash runway, and less if cash burn accelerates as overhead costs increase as they progress through a Phase 3 trial for REC-4881. The company will have to issue more shares for cash, further escalating the share dilution already endured. I see no other outcome.

On the surface, based on current, quick, and debt/equity ratios, the company looks well capitalized with a strong balance sheet, but only when factoring in share dilution and cash runway do we see some of the risks here.

Recursion recorded a net loss of $645 million in 2025, which was significantly more than the $464 million net loss the previous year. The company generated just $74.7 million in revenue in 2025, representing a 27% growth rate. This represented consistent growth and was more than some of their peers. However, this was far below the $111 million in stock-based compensation (SBC) they awarded themselves, which grew at an even higher rate (36%) from 2024. In the chart below, I have compared not just the amounts of revenue generated versus SBC awarded, but also the year-on-year growth rate of both. For 10 straight quarters, management has been willing to extend themselves extra stock options and at a faster pace of growth than they can increase revenue. This raises a red flag. I understand the need to retain talent, but what kind of message does this send shareholders? When management seem content to reward themselves generously for less than spectacular performance, what does this say?

In terms of valuation, companies like this are difficult to assess. As they are not generating any earnings or FCF, P/E ratio and price/FCF are useless as measures. Even P/S ratio is less than helpful given the company is in its early stage of growth, and PB ratios can be distorted when the company takes on lots of debt or sells equity. In any event, here are the figures for P/S and P/B from Seeking Alpha:

Certainly, the TAM in this industry is enormous, and this might represent a good place to consider valuation. The market research firm Markets and Markets predicts the AI-based clinical trials market could reach $2.74 billion by 2030 (representing a 12.5% CAGR), while Grand View Research are more bullish, predicting a 2030 industry value of $7.8 billion (22.1% CAGR). Fortune Business Insights predicts a whopping 41.7% CAGR. Clearly, these are wild guesses, and this may highlight part of the problem for investors. We simply do not know how much AI will penetrate this space, how many companies can “win” in this market, let alone who those winners could end up being. It would be speculative to assess where Recursion could end up being positioned in this space, as they have not yet proven they can generate meaningful revenue from both their drugs (biotech segment) and Recursion OS (partnership segment), let alone value for shareholders. To invest here is to take an enormous risk in my view, but certainly it could play out—"could” being the operative word.

Concluding Thoughts

Recursion has the potential to be a leading player in this exciting new space of AI-harnessed drug discovery. They have strong partnerships and treatments in their pipeline, yet they are several years away from meaningful revenue generation, and they are burning cash at an increasing rate each quarter. Shareholders will continue being punished with share dilution as a result.

Management will report earnings pre-market on May 5, and investors will want to focus on revenue generation from partnerships, milestone payments to highlight sustained backing from biotech players, and any new data read-outs or information on their pipeline to determine the likely timeline for extend clinical trials and approval. The former is the most important though, as the company will have to rely upon their partnerships to sustain themselves financially until they can generate revenue from their own pipeline.

With competition increasing in this space and Recursion unable to prove, at this point, what their competitive advantage is in this industry, I will retain my Hold rating. Certainly, the recent Nvidia equity sale looks bad for the company, and the progress seen from some of their peers (esp. InSilico, Generate Biomedicines, Isomorphic Labs, Relay Therapeutics and Schrödinger) in recent months may indicate that Recursion are falling behind in this race. At this juncture, with more questions than answers about their platform and potential business moat, I will remain on the sidelines and would not consider buying until they can show stronger evidence of how their platform is superior to their peers. Indeed, when looking across this landscape, there may be better investments among their peers. I would rate this a sell were it not for the industry’s enormous potential, which Recursion could tap into, if they can execute properly.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla: Disastrous Robotaxi Checkup
data.description:
In no real surprise here, Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) again pushed back the dream of a large robotaxi rollout. CEO Elon Musk even further dampened the plans for turning existing Tesla EVs into personal robotaxis while potentially opening up the company to major lawsuits. My investment thesis remains ultra Bearish on the stock as competitors increasingly move into the robotaxi space while the valuation doesn't add up.

Reality Check

For years now, CEO Elon Musk has highlighted a path to massive scale in the robotaxi race. Tesla was supposed to have this fleet of 10 million EVs, with production ramping up on millions of Cybercabs annually.

Tesla reported Q1 26 sales with revenues up 16% YoY to $22.4 billion, but one has to note the company reported higher March quarter sales of $23.3 billion all the way back in Q1 2023.

The real story is that Musk got on the earnings call and gave shareholders a major reality check on the robotaxi rollout. Going into the earnings report, investor excitement jumped with Tesla announcing the rollout of robotaxi services in Dallas and Houston, with Elon retweeting the announcement on April 18.

As usual, a big gap exists between the service offering and the promotion. At launch, Tesla had only one robotaxi vehicle in both areas and has only reached two over a week later.

When covering Tesla a few months ago, the Robotaxi Tracker site only listed Tesla as having 7 robotaxis (unsupervised) in Austin. Now, the company has just a total of 19 actual robotaxis in the 3 major cities in Texas.

Tesla can now claim official robotaxi services in 3 cities, while Austin still hasn't scaled or expanded to a large service area. In reality, Tesla has made no progress on launching robotaxi services at scale, and Musk further suggested the rollout won't occur until possibly next year.

On the Q1 2026 earnings call, CEO Elon Musk again pushed back with the release of FSD V15.

For Full Self-Driving and Robotaxi, Version 14.3 was a major architectural update. And we have a whole pipeline of major improvements to Full Self-Driving that, we believe, will lead to unsupervised Full Self-Driving being available anywhere in the world that it is legal to do so. And then there's a Version 15, which hopefully later this -- hopefully by the end of this year, but certainly by early next year. And that will be a complete overhaul of the software architecture and will run on AI4. That's -- and that -- at that point, we're really just increasing the safety level of FSD above human safety level even more, meaning I think even within Version 14, we're significantly safer than humans, but V15 will take that to another level.

We have a lot of known improvements, like major architectural improvements that we know would improve the probability of safety significantly. So I think it's not going to make sense for us to deploy unsupervised FSD or Robotaxi large scale when we know that there are major architectural improvements to the software that can improve safety.

So I think we're going to want to finish writing that software, validate it and release it before going to large-scale unsupervised FSD, depending on what large-scale means.

So despite all the promises of major robotaxi rollouts last year, Musk actually drops the reality check on shareholders that large-scale, unsupervised robotaxis won't start until FSD V15.

Back on the Q1 2026 earnings call, Musk further dampened the robotaxi rollout plans with the unexpected removal of launching some existing personal cars:

Unfortunately, Hardware 3 -- I wish it were otherwise, but Hardware 3 simply does not have the capability to achieve unsupervised FSD. We did think at one point it would have that, but relative to Hardware 4, it has only 1/8 of the memory bandwidth of Hardware 4. And memory bandwidth is one of the key elements needed for unsupervised FSD... And you also need to replace the cameras, unfortunately, to go to Hardware 4.

Anyone watching Tesla struggle to launch a real robotaxi service here in 2026 probably isn't surprised that EVs with old hardware aren't capable of operating the vehicle at Level 4 autonomy in the future. A big issue is the type of costs and liabilities Tesla faces after promising FSD offered this path to full autonomy and charging customers for years. On the earnings call, Elon Musk discussed standing up service centers to replace the Hardware 3 systems and cameras, but Tesla could also face lawsuits in addition to these costs of replacing equipment.

Even worse, Tesla doesn’t fully distinguish between paid Robotaxi business miles driven and actual robotaxi miles, which are a tiny fraction of total miles with only a few cars operating. Supervised miles in 2026 don’t matter anymore with competition launching.

Outside the internal Tesla issues with launching a real robotaxi business outside a few token unsupervised vehicles in Texas, the company faces real competitive threats now. Avride, owned by Nebius (NBIS), just announced the company has already reached a fleet of 200 AVs in Texas with robotaxi rides in Dallas on the Uber (UBER) platform.

The famous Robotaxi Tracker doesn't even include a section for Avride. Along with Zoox, owned by Amazon (AMZN), these new entrants into the robotaxi race aren't being tracked to the same degree as the EV giant, but these companies appear to be going full speed ahead on launching actual robotaxi services without any need to distinguish between a supervised or unsupervised service.

Tesla just started production of the Cybercab, so the company has the potential to ramp up services faster, but the market should have no confidence in this concept. The company should've launched full robotaxi services in the Austin market before shifting to both Dallas and Houston or even starting Cybercab production.

Sum Of Parts Doesn't Add Up

Tesla enters 2026 in a heavy capex phase. The company has major plans to spend on robotaxis, humanoid robot manufacturing, and AI chips, but the business is starting to lose perspective.

The company announced capital spending plans were jumping to $25 billion after announcing major plans to ramp up spending to $20 billion when reporting Q4 earnings. Tesla has gone nearly three years now without any material revenue growth with the focus on EV production, and a lot of the prior projects have left the auto business without fresh new EV models to boost sales.

What the market knows about the robotaxis business deals now requires investors to take a more realistic view on the valuation of the business. The domestic peers for robotaxis and humanoid robots have combined valuations nowhere near the one assigned to Tesla.

The domestic equivalent of Robotaxis is Waymo, owned by Alphabet (GOOG), with a recent capital raise at a $126 billion valuation. Figure AI is the humanoid robot equivalent, with a recent valuation of $39 billion.

These combined businesses are arguably ahead of Tesla and only have a combined valuation of $165 billion. Waymo is far ahead of Tesla in robotaxi operations, offering 500K rides per week now in 11 cities in the U.S. versus the minimal test vehicles for Tesla.

Gordon Johnson of GLJ Research offers this table with a sum-of-parts valuation for Tesla. The stock currently trades at $376, and this valuation works out to only $54.

As the above example provides, Ford Motor (F) is only worth $50 billion, while Toyota Motor (TM), with over $300 billion in annual sales, is just worth $250 billion. Even using the far bigger and more profitable Toyota Motor valuation for the Car Biz, the sum-of-parts valuation just tops $400 billion.

Based on these valuations, the market is assigning over $1 trillion of additional market cap to the robotaxi and humanoid robots business, while the business appears behind in market execution to the private firms. Based on the above info regarding the robotaxi business, Tesla is behind Waymo and is facing mounting competitive pressure and has already lost the first mover advantage needed to warrant a massive premium.

Tesla currently has a $1.4 trillion valuation with a 2026 revenue target of ~$100 billion. The stock trades at 179x 2026 EPS targets and 150x 2027 targets.

The major risk is Tesla finally being valued based on actual financial results, with the venture arms (robotaxis, humanoids) of the business valued like peers. One could argue the Tesla robotaxi business isn't worth anywhere close to Waymo at over $129 billion, suggesting the whole sum-of-the-parts valuation wouldn't match the $54 estimate.

The major risk to bears is that Tesla has a clear path to justifying the current valuation by finally launching a robotaxi service at a large scale. The Cybercab production plans would give the company an advantage over other robotaxi services without internal manufacturing. If Tesla got to millions of robotaxis in service before the competition, the market valuation could reach an Ark Invest target.

The Optimus humanoid robot offers the same ability to convert an existing EV manufacturing facility in Fremont, CA into a robotics hub. The problem remains that Tesla constantly pushes back timelines while competitors execute.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Tesla just dashed any expectation for a scaled robotaxi service for up to a year. The stock currently trades as if the EV manufacturer dominates the sector, while signs are emerging of Tesla slipping in the race.

Investors shouldn't touch Tesla at this valuation while facing major competitive threats. Even solid execution isn't likely to justify the current valuation for years.

data.title: Markets Price In End Of Iran Conflict, S&P 500 May Climb Higher
data.description:
Introduction

After the market recovered in record time after a 9% drop driven by the Iran war, I believe we are ready to reach new highs on the S&P 500 (SP500). It was one of the quickest recoveries in 36 years.

The conflict in the Middle East, which began with the US attack on February 28, drove stock prices down, while oil prices reached a high of $115 on April 6. But we, as investors, have adapted to volatility, as well as to the constant announcements from Donald Trump, who declared the war was over.

The first time he declared in that way was March 9th, when he said Iran was running out of military forces to fight back. Crude oil prices sharply declined that day. I remember buying the US Oil Fund ETF (USO) days before, and I was very surprised when I saw such a sharp drop in my portfolio. Oil entered a cycle of high volatility, but in April my outlook changed. I sold my USO shares at the end of March with a moderate profit and positioned my portfolio for the end of the war, maintaining my investment style and resuming my long-term projections.

I believe the end of the war is already priced in by the market. But this doesn't mean the war has actually ended. But Trump's posts on X seem to age so quickly that the market no longer reacts to them the way it once did. This dynamic became more pronounced since April 8th, when the first ceasefire was confirmed, which was later followed by Iran's announcement of the opening of the Strait of Hormuz. I think VIX clearly shows this turning point, as it hasn't exceeded 20 points since then. From that volatility drop, the S&P 500 got back to all-time highs, reaching 7,165 points on Friday.

The Drivers of the Markets

The first two months of 2026 were already flat due to factors unrelated to the war. I believe those factors were the high valuation of technology companies, the big rotation towards defensive sectors, and AI-driven disruption in the software industry. I think those factors created the conditions for a major decline in the face of an external event: the conflict in the Middle East.

But now we have to pay attention to what's coming. And I'm very optimistic. I estimate a target of 8,000 points for the S&P 500 in 2026. I wrote my thoughts on how far the market could drop in this article. I said the market would fall no more than 8 or 10%, even in the worst-case scenario: a troop deployment in Iran. So, I started looking at the fundamentals of the economy in the long term, the earnings of some companies, strong investment in AI, and the monetary policy of the Fed, and I ended up with a bullish opinion. I'll explain those four factors.

1. Economic Boost

Despite the rise in crude oil prices and damage to energy infrastructure that could keep prices high for longer, the economic outlook remained largely unchanged. The IMF projects GDP growth of 2.3% versus the 2.4% expected in January. The underlying assumptions in the estimate have an average crude oil price of $82 in 2026 and an average yield of 3.5% on 3-month bonds.

The latest activity data were positive in April, with the S&P Global Composite PMI at 52.0 compared to the consensus of 50.5. The services PMI also beat expectations, coming in at 54.0 versus the 52.5 consensus. Services also follow the increase, and both are rebounding from 50.0, as you can see in the image.

But this could quickly change. I mean, it's just a preliminary photo. But it could get worse if energy prices rise more than expected. But I'll talk about that later. I'd also like to mention a tax incentive that is sometimes forgotten regarding the OBBBA, which could help soften the impact on consumers.

2. Exceptional Corporate Profits

We are once again in earnings season, and companies are showing extraordinary results. As of April 24, 2026, 28% of S&P 500 companies reported Q1 2026 earnings. 84% reported EPS above consensus estimates, exceeding the five-year average of 76%. Something similar happens with revenue. 81% of companies reported revenue above consensus estimates, surpassing the five-year average of 70%.

The technology sector continues to lead with profits that grew 46% YoY. From the Magnificent 7, only Tesla (TSLA) has reported results, and it comfortably outperformed the market consensus. Behind the technology sector were materials (33%) and financials (19%). The combined earnings growth rate for the entire index was 15.1%, exceeding the 13.1% estimated at the end of March.

In summary, I think it makes sense that economic activity estimates remain solid. In general, companies are successfully monetizing their investments and earning more money. And that's why the economy keeps growing on solid fundamentals. Anyway, as I've said before, this is a preliminary view, and we still need to look at the earnings of most companies.

3. The Creative Destruction of AI

CAPEX allocated to AI development in 2026 is projected to reach $667 billion, up from $527 billion as of last December. This would represent 1.5% CAPEX growth in 2026 and nearly 0.2% of GDP. But the main incentive of AI is reaching a bigger profitability by reducing or eliminating expensive tasks.

This is what Joseph Alois Schumpeter called creative destruction: that capital can move to more productive sectors. Yes, some businesses may close their doors along the way, but at the end, there will be a more efficient economy. There are a lot of sectors affected. For example, 30% of software development jobs could disappear. The same happens with customer support services. That's why I believe AI infrastructure, being in full development, will continue to be a key driver of the economy. And I think those who bet on this trend will benefit greatly.

4. Monetary Policy

The Fed paused its rate-cutting cycle that began in 2024. In 2025, rates were cut by 75 basis points, from 4.5% to 3.75%. With the conflict in the Middle East, the rise of crude oil, and the unemployment rate of 4.3%-4.5%, I think the pause in rate cuts was already priced in by the market, which had estimated 1 or 2 more cuts for 2026 and corrected accordingly.

Now, the expectation is that there won't be any cuts this year. Is this necessarily bad news? I believe it is not. On the contrary, I think it's actually good news, because inflation expectations have worsened. So, with a worse outlook, I believe the economy is comfortable at rate levels of 3.50%-3.75%. I believe this is a neutral rate. If they raise it again, that would be an adverse signal and a new risk, which I'll address later.

How Many Times Did the Market Rise by Double Digits for 4 Consecutive Years?

So, if the S&P 500 indeed reaches 8,000 points, that would imply a gain of 15%-17% for the year and would be the fourth consecutive year with a rise of two digits. Is that possible? What does history tell us about that? Those were some questions I asked myself when I concluded the market would resume an attractive bullish trend.

It has only happened three times in history to have four consecutive years of growth in the S&P 500. Two of those instances occurred before and after World War II. The last historical instance of four consecutive years of market gains was between 1995 and 1999, a period that lasted five years. During that time, there was strong growth in technology companies, although the dot-com bubble was already building up, which ultimately burst between 1999 and 2000, followed by a 9% correction the next year.

The other period that almost achieved something similar was from 2011 to 2015, although it represented more of a market recovery after the major real estate and financial crisis of 2008, in a context of 0% interest rates and, in my opinion, an excessively expansionary monetary policy. In 2022 the streak was broken again by the sharp rise in interest rates following a monetary policy that was, once again, excessively expansionary due to the pandemic.

I believe 2026 has solid fundamentals to support a fourth consecutive year of double-digit gains. I would even compare the current period with 1995-1990. Mainly because today we have a disruptive tech tool, AI, which I believe can be compared with the revolution caused by web browsers such as Yahoo and the boom in personal computer sales before 2000.

The only difference I see between 2023-2026 and 1995-1999 is that there isn't a bubble, at least not yet. I understand a bubble as a disconnect between a company's underlying fundamentals and earnings versus its stock price. There was a clear disconnect in the dot-com bubble. Something I just don't see today.

In summary, I believe the market is on track for a fourth consecutive year of a rise of two digits. I don't see any runaway variables in the macroeconomy. And to add some context to the S&P 500's performance in recent years, I've added some key macroeconomic metrics with my S&P 500 estimation for 2026, while the GDP growth and inflation rate forecasts are from the IMF.

How to Position Yourself for New Highs? Technology, but with Selectivity

As the S&P 500 is mostly made up of technology stocks, with a 33% weighting, I consider it prudent to have exposure to stocks within this sector. However, not every stock is an opportunity. The technology sector (XLK) (QQQ) is enormous, and many businesses are being threatened by the disruption of AI. One of those threatened businesses is software. Some of the main software companies are grouped in IGV, which has dropped 20% so far this year.

I already mentioned in previous articles that I have a long-term position in Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and that I recently added a little more during the pullback from its highs. Its Azure business looks more than solid to me in the long term, while its earnings per share are expected to grow at a double-digit rate over the next five years. For a company of MSFT's size and scale, I find that more than attractive. Anyway, I'm becoming less enthusiastic, not about MSFT, but about the doubts in the sector.

Microsoft will report earnings next Wednesday and expects revenue of $81.4 billion and EPS of $4.06, growing 16% and 17% YoY, respectively. The stock recovered this month and cleared some of my fears. But I'm not much more enthusiastic about similar companies. Here are the top companies by market capitalization in applications and software systems.

I know many are tempted to buy on dips, but I would be careful with stocks that have been falling for a while. I'm talking about Adobe (ADBE), Salesforce (CRM), ServiceNow (NOW), and Intuit (INTU), for example. I recently wrote an article on ADBE explaining my perspective in detail.

Some of those risks can be avoided by rotating out of the software sector and into stocks that support AI development, such as semiconductors (SMH) (PSI). NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) still leads the market, and its profitability still seems impressive to me, with gross margins of 71% over the last 12 months, even above its 5-year average of 67%. NVDA's EPS could grow by almost 75% this year. And although the stock reached an all-time high on Friday, its P/E ratio of 42x is below the 73x of the last 5 years. Here's the thing: the company with the largest capitalization of the S&P 500, worth nearly $5 trillion, shows some clear signs of undervaluation.

I'm very optimistic about Nvidia and the whole semiconductor sector. Another one of my favorite stocks is Celestica (CLS), which I rate a strong buy after the Q4 2025 earnings presentation. Since then, the stock has risen 39%. CLS is a key manufacturer of the switches that optimize the operation of semiconductors. And without semiconductors, there is no AI development. But CLS is known for the speed of its 400G and 800G network switches and now expects to bring 16T switches to market, doubling efficiency. The company expects EPS growth of nearly 49% in 2026 and could potentially double by 2028.

A Contrasting View: Why Might It Be Wrong?

To be fair, I must say that I could be wrong. Analysts are wrong a lot more often than it seems. And we are wrong because we are human beings. My thesis carries clear macroeconomic risks, especially given the fears around recession and inflation. Goldman Sachs estimates there is a 30% probability of a recession this year and annual inflation of 5% in the worst-case scenario.

So, a bear analyst would say that economic problems will affect the earnings expectations of the biggest companies. For example, if crude oil prices stay elevated over the long term, it will affect the whole supply chain. So, just as I said that the OBBBA's tax incentives improve the economy, the prolonged increase in gasoline prices worsens the economy by reducing consumers' purchasing power. The rise of crude oil causes inflation to rise, and this could also complicate monetary policy and the possibility of lowering interest rates more quickly.

A bearish view should anticipate interest rates rising back to the 4%-4.5% range by the end of 2026 if Goldman Sachs' worst-case scenario comes true. That could indeed affect tech companies in the S&P 500, causing a temporary pullback in the markets. I believe in that scenario, the S&P 500 could return to its recent lows in the 6,300-6,400 point range. I believe it's unlikely that the Fed will raise interest rates to 4.5% in 2026, and I even think it's unlikely they'll raise them at all in 2026. I think it is more likely to see a 25 bps increase in 2026. I also don't believe that would be enough to change the bullish trend of the market. Anyway, Kevin Warsh, who could be the new Fed chairman, might suggest that we are heading toward a higher-rate environment, potentially triggering a market correction.

Summary

I believe the market is already pricing in a potential end to the conflict with Iran and a gradual reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, which could help normalize global oil logistics. The recent all-time highs in the S&P 500 support this idea.

But now I've become more optimistic. I think a new upside has been unlocked, with 8,000 points as the next target. And this could be reached due to four main factors: solid economic momentum, strong corporate earnings, continued growth in AI, and a neutral monetary policy.

Your time is more valuable than any investment decision. Thank you for taking the time to read this article

data.title: BP: A New CEO, A Shareholder Revolt, And A Gap To Fill
data.description:
BP p.l.c. (BP) is trading at $46.25 with an open area gap below at $44.78, and I think the gap will fill within the next two weeks. The setup is straightforward: a new CEO who has been on the job for less than a month, a shareholder revolt that just embarrassed the board at Thursday's AGM, earnings on Tuesday (April 28) with elevated uncertainty, and a consensus analyst target of $44.14 that already sits below the current price. When the analysts covering a stock think it's going down, and the chart agrees, that's the kind of alignment I'm happy to trade.

The Gap

BP gapped up through the $44.78 level in mid-April on the Iran ceasefire optimism that briefly lifted energy stocks. Brent crude jumped on hopes that Strait of Hormuz tensions would de-escalate, and BP rode the wave. The gap at $44.78 was left behind.

BP is usually a good stock to gap-trade on, as most gaps within the trading range fill (in both directions!). On that note, the $44.78 gap is an unfilled area gap. My backtests across thousands of area gaps show fill rates above 85%, with the median fill occurring within five to ten trading days. The gap-fill target of $44.78 represents about 3% downside from the current price, which is modest in absolute terms but highly tradeable with options leverage.

The catalyst for the fill is already here: earnings on April 28 with a CEO who has been on the job for under a month.

The Leadership Vacuum

BP is now on its fourth CEO in six years. Bernard Looney resigned. Murray Auchincloss lasted two years before stepping down. Carol Howle served as interim. And now Meg O'Neill, formerly of Woodside Energy, took over on April 1.

That's a revolving door. And each CEO has brought a fundamentally different strategy. Looney bet big on renewables. Auchincloss reversed course, slashed renewable investment by 70%, and pivoted back to oil and gas under pressure from Elliott Management. O'Neill is expected to double down on upstream, but she hasn't had time to put her stamp on anything yet. TD Cowen's energy analyst put it best: changing your strategy three times in five years is never a good thing.

Meanwhile, the AGM on Thursday was a disaster. Shareholders voted down two management-backed resolutions, including one that would have revoked climate disclosure obligations from 2015 and 2019. Both received only 47% support against a 75% threshold. Chairman Albert Manifold scraped through with 82% approval on his re-election, a near-record low for a UK major. For context, his predecessor, Helge Lund, received 96% in 2024. LGIM, the UK's largest asset manager, voted against Manifold. This is not a company with a unified shareholder base behind its strategy.

The Valuation

The consensus analyst target is $44.14. That's below the current price. Nine analysts covering a stock collectively think it's going to decline, and the target happens to sit right near my gap-fill level of $44.78. I.e., the technical and fundamental cases are saying the same thing.

BP also suspended share buybacks in early 2026 to prioritize hitting a sub-$20 billion net debt target. For a company with a track record of returning capital aggressively, pulling the buyback lever removes one of the key support mechanisms for the stock price. The dividend yield at 4.3% is attractive, but yield alone doesn't prevent a stock from filling a 3% gap, especially heading into an uncertain earnings print.

Risks

Oil Price Surge

If the Iran situation escalates again and Brent pushes meaningfully above $90, BP goes up regardless of the gap or the governance issues. Commodity price is the dominant variable for this stock, and it can overwhelm any technical or fundamental thesis. I'm not a commodity guy, so please do consult your favorite oil analyst before engaging in this trade.

Earnings Beat

BP's Q1 trading statement flagged "exceptional" oil trading performance in the quarter, with Brent averaging $81.13/bbl vs. $63.73 in Q4. A strong beat on Tuesday could send the stock higher and prolong (or even invalidate) the gap trade.

New CEO Delivers Strong First Impression

O'Neill could surprise the market with a decisive strategic announcement alongside earnings. Her track record at Woodside, including the BHP Petroleum acquisition, gives her credibility with upstream-focused investors. Maybe she really is the hero that BP needs and presents a new, inspiring vision for BP, driving shareholders to cease their revolts in solidarity with the company. (As I typed that sentence, I felt as if I were saying, "This time is different," and we traders/investors all know the implications of that phrase, namely that, no, this time is indeed not different).

The Play

I'm buying the May 16 $46 puts. At current premiums, these should cost roughly $1.65 per contract. These are right at the money, which gives you strong delta exposure to the downside move from the jump. At the gap-fill target of $44.78, these puts are $1.22 in the money at expiration, and the delta movement from $46.25 to $44.78 combined with any IV expansion around earnings should push the value to over $4, depending on speed.

And, to clarify, by "speed," I mean the velocity of the stock movement, not the option greek. In other words, if BP fills the gap in three days rather than ten, you keep more extrinsic value on the puts because theta hasn't eaten as much of the premium. A fast fill also tends to coincide with higher IV, which inflates the put further.

Putting all this together: If BP fills the gap within a week of earnings, which my backtests suggest is the most probable timeline for an area gap of this type, you're looking at a 50-80% ROI on a trade with a defined max loss of the premium paid.

You are now reading my concluding sentence that sums up everything I've written above, thereby invalidating all the reading you've done thus far should you not care about the specifics, though I do encourage you to care about the specifics: An area gap below, a brand-new CEO facing her first earnings call, a hostile shareholder base, analysts who think the stock is going down, and no buyback support all point to a short trade, for which I'll be buying the May $46 puts.

I hope you like the trade. Remember: It's important to have faith in people, especially yourself. Thank you for reading my idea, and please have fun trading because it sure can be a lot of fun.

data.title: Tesla: What Most Don't Know About FSD
data.description:
It is common for Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) investors to think that Tesla has a unique advantage in its FSD (Full Self-Driving) technology and that the competition is hopelessly outclassed, sitting many years behind Tesla.

There is also, on the other hand, among the Tesla skeptics, the idea that FSD, unless it reaches its "Unsupervised" status, is not very useful. This article will address both misconceptions, alongside a lot of further information that's either not well-known or spread across the Internet in obscure places.

The "Unique FSD" Misconception By Tesla Fans

When people hear about Chinese EVs, they mostly focus on the designs, the luxury, the impossibly low prices, and the technology. But they miss something.

They miss that, among many other things, the already-available Chinese EV competitors also have alternative systems to Tesla's FSD, and these alternative systems are competitive with the FSD version Tesla is allowed to sell in China (examples I, II, and III of recent tests and reviews).

You see, Tesla is only able to sell v13.2.9 in China. This is a version Tesla launched in the U.S. on May 13, 2025. So less than 1 year ago. This version is, of course, significantly behind what Tesla offers in the U.S. right now (on HW4): v14.3.2. However, v13.2.9 is already competent to drive itself (under supervision) in urban streets, park itself alone, etc. Notice, indeed, that v13.2.9 is already well ahead of the best FSD version available to Tesla HW3 owners.

It so happens that the alternatives to FSD in China, available from many brands including those connected to Huawei, plus Xpeng (XPEV), NIO (NIO), Xiaomi (XIACF), a host of brands using Momenta's R6 model, etc., now routinely match or outperform Tesla's "available in China" FSD. Tesla no longer dominates in this regard in China because it stood still there. And therein lies the problem -- these alternatives are matching or outperforming a Tesla FSD version launched less than one year ago!

That these alternatives exist and are able to achieve such performance means that several Chinese competitors, including broad suppliers which also supply Western brands, are less than 1 year behind Tesla on FSD tech at this moment. It's immediately obvious that even if Tesla achieves "Unsupervised" status, there's no reason to believe several others, centered in China, won't achieve the same 6-12 months after.

This, by the way, mirrors what's happening in AI in general, where Chinese companies keep showing advanced AI systems which trail only the leading US closed models by 6-12 months. This is happening across text, reasoning, audio, image generation, and video (where, arguably, a Chinese model, Seedance 2.0, actually leads the pack).

Given this reality, we can safely say that Tesla's fan base is simply wrong in thinking that FSD is an unassailable Tesla advantage and that others are hopelessly behind. They aren't. Only this isn't visible in the West because, for now, these alternatives are confined to the Chinese market. But of course, both through supplier relationships and through foreign expansion of the Chinese brands, these alternatives to FSD are certain to hit Western markets in the near future.

A Further Comment Is Needed

Something Tesla fans also don't understand is that while Tesla charges for the vehicle to be equipped with FSD, or (now) a monthly subscription to use FSD, in China this is a lot less common.

Some systems in China still involve some upfront payment or subscription, typically much cheaper than Tesla. Huawei-linked systems typically fall in this case. However, many, like Nio's, Xpeng's, Xiaomi's, or even those based on Momenta R6, are typically bundled in the car price, even though that price is below to well below Tesla's own car pricing (without FSD) for equivalent or superior models.

This means that FSD in China, both in capability and especially in cost, is a disadvantage for Tesla already.

The "FSD Is Useless Unless Supervised" Misconception By Tesla Skeptics

There is arguably also a misconception regarding Tesla's FSD running around the Tesla skeptics' circles. This is the idea that FSD will never reach (or it will take many years) "Unsupervised" status, and thus FSD is worthless.

This is a misconception because FSD, even "Supervised," is still already a very, very useful feature. Obviously, it requires careful supervision, or it might present a safety hazard at some point. But, in its present state, FSD as a driver assistance system can go for up to hundreds of km without requiring intervention, even in urban environments. Hence, in its present state, FSD already meaningfully reduces the stress of driving, particularly in boring (but simultaneously dangerous) circumstances like long-distance driving or driving in a congested, very active, urban zone.

Since FSD is already so useful, it presents itself as a powerful sales argument. And as such, it cannot possibly be ignored by the skeptics or cast away just because it's not "Unsupervised" yet.

A Further Comment Is Needed

A "good enough" driver assistance might present a challenge for Tesla as long as FSD remains "Unsupervised." Sure, Tesla's system is presently superior (in its v14 versions), including against all Chinese competitors. However, as long as the competition isn't obviously bad, there isn't a huge difference between always having to be attentive to catch 1 needed intervention in 1,000km, versus to catch 2, 3, 5, 10. Attention is always required either way.

This "good enough" concept means competent-enough driver assistance systems will still compete on cost, at least until one of them reaches "Unsupervised" status. And even then, as explained before, that advantage likely won't last for a long time.

A Weird (Current) Chinese Advantage

There's also a reason to think the Chinese competitors might be able to catch up further to Tesla. The reason is simple: while the Chinese competitors lag Tesla substantially in terms of compute available to train their models, they actually beat Tesla substantially in terms of inference computing available in their cars.

Cars having advanced driver assistance in China now routinely ship with 1,000-3,000 TOPS in compute. This compares with 500-720 on the HW4 Teslas. Momenta R6-equipped cars, or the lowest-end Chinese cars, tend to ship with Tesla-comparable compute, though. On memory available for inference, the Chinese also typically equip 16Gb-64Gb per chip, which is less disparate with Tesla's HW4 (16Gb per chip). And indeed, Tesla, on the HW4.1/Plus, which is arriving, will be shipping 32Gb per chip (64Gb total). This, though, does create problems in the sense that some HW4 cars will be substantially more competent than others (due to having more memory per chip).

The available car inference and memory put a limit on how performant and large the models can be in the car. Larger, faster models to be inferred on the car have an advantage over smaller ones, everything else being equal.

Tesla did recently proclaim that HW5 (AI5) has taped out. HW5 could theoretically make the previously described current Chinese advantage disappear, given the large expected increase in performance for HW5 versus HW4.

However, a problem remains. For a chip as complex as AI5, the time between tape-out and it arriving in products can be expected to be around a year. And of course, the Chinese aren't standing still -- the advantage we talked about is what exists now, not in 1-2 years. Hence, by the time Tesla starts bringing AI5 to its cars, the Chinese competitors will likely also be fielding solutions superior to those they field today and will either match or exceed AI5 again.

As an aside, an interesting speculation of mine is that Elon Musk probably got nervous when he became aware of the time it would still take for AI5 to arrive after it was "complete." That would explain the fascination with a fab where everything would be faster, with the steps that would specifically be optimized being precisely those that stand between the tape-out and shipping the product.

Liability Once (If) FSD Unsupervised Arrives

There's a common issue running among the Tesla community (fans and skeptics alike) regarding the liability for accidents once FSD Unsupervised arrives.

This is actually an easy issue to infer. In my opinion, liability will jump automatically to Tesla if and when Unsupervised arrives. Indeed, such will happen even if Tesla doesn't state it directly, and if Tesla states it directly, then that's sufficient proof to say Unsupervised FSD arrived.

The reason is simple. With FSD Unsupervised, the customer doesn't need to monitor. He doesn't even need to be present. It's a logical consequence that ANY accident or issue happening while FSD Unsupervised was activated is a result either of someone else's fault or a system failure. When stuff breaks in your car (other than for lack of maintenance), it's always the responsibility of the car's manufacturer -- and it wouldn't be different with FSD (as long as the system is used within conditions set by Tesla, but if outside those conditions, the car would have to immobilize itself, as the customer couldn't even be assumed to be inside, never mind monitoring the conditions).

Why Is FSD So Important For Tesla Right Now?

Tesla has quit deploying new car models. In the automotive world, and particularly against competent competitors, to stop is to die. It's hard to sell a car as it grows more and more obsolete. And the current Model 3 and Model Y are, indeed, mostly obsolete except for FSD. Of course, this is more evident in China than elsewhere.

In China, these Tesla models:

  • Have an obsolete electrical architecture (400v, when no new mid- to high-end models ship without 800v or higher nowadays)
  • Charge much slower (mostly as a result of the obsolete electrical architecture not giving access to the latest battery advancements)
  • Have a lower range (than comparable models)
  • Have lower performance (than comparable models)
  • Have older designs
  • Have weaker interiors (than comparable models)
  • Have less sophisticated suspensions (than comparable models)
  • And so on and so forth.

They hold themselves as commercially viable mostly on Tesla's brand strength, as well as aggressive promotions, alongside (diminishing) fears about Chinese competitors' quality and service limitations.

Still, with each day that passes, the competitive environment gets harder for Tesla. Its sales in China are flat to down versus 2-3 years ago in a growing market, and Tesla is now vulnerable to a fast sales decline.

What happened in China is also spreading (slower) elsewhere. Both Chinese brands expanding outside China and legacy foreign brands putting pressure on Tesla are a reality, as they, too, are evolving towards the specs broadly available in China.

Of course, and this is why FSD is so important; outside China, FSD still stands unchallenged. That, of course, creates the impression on Tesla fans that alternatives don't exist for FSD outside Tesla. But, as I've shown, they do exist; they're just not yet spreading worldwide (but they will, short of the protectionist U.S. market).

Of course, barring a substantial, structural advantage, there's no way Tesla will be able to keep its absurd earnings multiple (or even to grow sales numbers). This advantage, though, as we saw, doesn't exist. The threat is just contained to China right now, but it will spread very soon.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Tesla fans are factually wrong in thinking Tesla has an unassailable technological advantage with FSD. Several FSD alternatives exist already (in currently available EVs) which are factually less than 1 year away from Tesla's most advanced FSD versions available in the U.S. These alternatives are shrinking their lag and will be spreading outside China soon.

Tesla skeptics are also wrong in thinking FSD won't reach Unsupervised status (but might be right on timing taking longer), and especially they're wrong on FSD Supervised not being relevant -- it is relevant, and it is good enough to be a powerful feature and sales argument right now.

Tesla isn't launching new models, and older models are becoming obsolete. This will result in increasing sales pressure both in China and, slower, elsewhere.

Tesla stock is priced for perfection, and when it comes to selling cars, Tesla is now very, very far from perfection. It's likely that its earnings pressure will only intensify, likely to the point where Tesla will be posting losses.

Typically, what happens when a car manufacturer has only older models is that it has to discount them more and more heavily to "move metal." Tesla has already been doing this and is doing it more and more -- this last quarter (Q1 2026), Tesla was even selling the Model 3 and Y standard in Europe at Chinese prices (once taxes are stripped out). Practicing Chinese prices after transportation costs and tariffs means the margins on these vehicles were much lower than in China, if positive at all. The Q1 2026 earnings report by Tesla obscured this by not excluding 2 large ($480 million in total) one-off gains from tariff returns and a warranty reserve release.

Finally, even if Tesla's FSD reaches the "Unsupervised" level, it's rationally obvious that several (Chinese) competitors will reach the same within 1 year. As such, no massive value can be ascribed to something which will be broadly a competitive market within 1 year after Tesla reaches Unsupervised FSD. This means Tesla's overvaluation and model obsolescence cannot be compensated by FSD, and a reckoning will eventually happen.

data.title: Intel: Redrawing The CPU AI Map (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC) has surged 77.6% since I upgraded the stock from Hold to Buy earlier this year. The stock price has reached my base case price target of $66.52 and nearly hit the more optimistic price target of $86.44. Intel’s surge is driven by Q1 results that exceeded expectations and redrawing the map on CPU usage in AI. In this report, I discuss the company’s earnings and explain why I am downgrading the stock from Buy to Hold.

Intel Beats Conservative Guidance

In my prior report, I already noted that Intel was setting relatively low targets for the subsequent quarter, and Q1 was no different other than the results being substantially higher than the guidance compared to guidance beats in the prior quarters. The company’s revenues grew 7.2% to $13.6 billion, which was $1.4 billion above the guidance and $1.15 billion above analyst estimates. Gross margins expanded 1.8 points to 41% and exceeded the estimates by 6.5 points. Normalized earnings per share of $0.29 were also sharply higher than expected, as Intel had guided for break-even earnings per share and analysts had modest expectations of $0.01.

A Deeper Look At Intel Earnings

Intel’s Client Computing Group remained Intel’s largest contributor of revenues with $7.7 billion. However, this marked a 6% decline sequentially and 1.3% growth year-on-year. We note that this market is actually in a challenging spot. Due to high PC component prices and likely overall economic uncertainty, we are seeing pressures on PC sales, and Intel expects low-double-digit declines for the market this year. Bright spots are the AI PCs now accounting for over 60% of the CPU mix, with 8% sequential growth recorded during the quarter and margins expanding 5.6 points sequentially and 1.7 points year-on-year to 32.6%.

Unsurprisingly, Intel’s Data Center & AI business had the strongest momentum, with sales growth of 22% year-over-year and 7% sequentially, driven by demand for Xeon server CPUs with workloads shifting from training toward inference and agentic AI, for which CPUs play a more important role. ASICs revenues grew 30% sequentially and are currently on a $1 billion annualized run rate. That is important because we are seeing that hyperscalers are splitting purchases between ASICs and general GPUs, where ASICs have the advantage that their custom designs make them more cost-efficient, and that is key to efficient scaling. Profits increased from $0.6 billion to $1.5 billion, with margins expanding from around 14% to 30.5%.

Intel Foundry posted revenues of $5.4 billion, which was a 20% sequential increase and 15% year-on-year. The segment, however, remained loss-making, with year-on-year losses increasing from $2.3 billion to $2.4 billion and from $2.5 billion to $2.4 billion sequentially. We do note that Intel is currently ramping up on Intel 18A and is seeing improved output on Intel 7, 4, and 3. So, that is helping the losses taper somewhat, but this is then partially offset by investment in Intel 14A. So, the sequential increase is not purely a reflection of yield improvements on Intel 18A but also carries ramping costs for 14A. We also note that Intel remains its biggest customer for Foundry, with external revenues only being $174 million. So, this is where Intel has opportunities, but it is currently working on a dual ramp-up on 18A and 14A, and only once we see yields at mature levels are the external revenues likely to pick up.

Intel Guidance Points At Declines

For the second quarter, Intel expects $13.8-$14.8 million in revenues, marking 11% growth at the midpoint. Margins are expected to expand strongly by 9.3 points to 39%, and EPS will flip from a $0.10 loss to a $0.20 profit.

Intel Comments On CPU In AI Are Bullish

The results that Intel presented as well as its guidance are impressive. However, what actually got me most excited was their comment on CPU importance for AI. By architecture, GPUs cannot replace CPUs, but so far we have seen that most growth is skewed towards GPUs, which is exactly what Intel does not provide.

Intel provided the following comment on the CPU-to-GPU ratio:

So first of all, I think the feedback from the customer, CPU is very important when you move from training to inference. Inference side, I think in terms of orchestration, control plane and also managing all the different agent with data, CPU is much more efficient. So I think the ratio of CPU to GPU used to be 1 and 8, and now it's 1:4 and I think towards parity or even better. So I think the demand is very strong.

It points at CPU demand actually getting better with new steps in AI.
