We went from training of language learning models, which was GPU-heavy, to inference, which already required more CPUs, and the comments that Intel provided point at further applications such as agentic AI driving CPU-to-GPU ratios even closer to parity. Essentially it directly supports Intel’s franchise and reinforces the durability of the x86 ecosystem.

Intel Stock Downgraded To Hold

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Intel, the expectation is that margins will continue to improve sharply in the years ahead. However, we also note that free cash flow will only turn positive in 2027 and the cash conversion ratio is still poor. It is a lot better than what it used to be, but Intel still has a very long way to go to get to acceptable levels. The positive is that the expectation is that Intel will see revenues growing in the years ahead after stagnant revenues from 2023 until 2025. Sales are expected to grow nearly 10% annually with 24% annual EBITDA growth and free cash flow still negative this year but getting to a positive position in 2027 and growing thereafter.

The results have been impressive, and the outlook also is impressive. However, in my view, at the company’s 3-year EV/EBITDA median of 18.3x, the stock is now fully valued. We do note that there is still upside when valuing the stock in line with peers. However, I do not believe that Intel deserves that peer group valuation at this stage. The outlook is much better today, but Intel has a long way to go to be really worth a peer group valuation in my view. As a result, I am downgrading the stock to Hold.

Conclusion: Intel Stock Is Now Fully Valued

Intel’s results were good, and primarily the need for more CPUs per GPU in the future is a positive sign. However, after the significant jump in the stock price, I do not believe there currently is an extremely attractive entry point when weighing the valuation against the execution risks that the company still faces.

data.title: Perspective Therapeutics: Decent Prospect In Radiopharmaceuticals Field
data.description:
Investment Overview - Explaining Perspective's Stock Surge In 2026

Perspective Therapeutics (CATX) is a radiopharmaceuticals specialist that went public in February 2023, via the merger of Nasdaq listed Isoray Inc., and privately held Viewpoint Molecular Targeting.

In early 2021 and mid-2024 Perspective stock achieved short-lived share price highs of >$15 per share, but a couple of underwhelming data readouts - most notably from a Phase 1/2a clinical trial of its candidate VMT-α-NET, in patients with unresectable or metastatic SSTR2-positive neuroendocrine tumors ("NETs") - dropped the share price back to ~$3.

In January this year, however, stock began to climb rapidly in value, rising from ~$2.5 per share, to >$4 per share, likely due to news that French Pharma giant Sanofi (SNY) had opted to discontinue its SSTR2-targeted radioimmunotherapy agent, Alphamedix, which it had suggested only months before it planned to enter into a Phase 3 clinical study in gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumours (GEP-NETs).

As we can see above (source: Perspective investor presentation), Perspective continues to develop its SSTR2 candidate targeting NETs, as well as a melanoma and a solid tumor candidate.

CEO Johan Spoor presented at the JP Morgan Healthcare conference last month, calling radiopharmaceuticals the "third pillar" of cancer treatment, alongside chemotherapy and immuno-oncology.

The CEO explained:

If something shows up on a tumor cell, but nowhere else, then we can target it. If we can target it, we can then cause a lot of damage there. And the great thing is it's a validated modality. It's becoming a mainstay. There's growth in the business. What we do is actually having a platform approach to this. These technologies link together. We use a proprietary chemical chelator that actually has been designed for superiority in biodistribution. And so we optimize our medicines to have the best possible biodistribution. We deliver really potent alpha particle payload. Betas and alphas are fundamentally different from each other. You need about 1,500 betas to kill a cancer cell. A single alpha particle can kill a cancer cell.

It is a complex mechanism of action ("MoA"), explained in more detail in Perspective's presentation, but it essentially involves an alpha emitting lead isotope, proprietary lead chelator (chemical compound that binds tightly to metal ions, forming a stable, ring-like structure), and engineered peptides for tumor specificity.

In January Perspective shared updated results from its 25 patient study of VMT-α-NET as follows:

  • 19 of the 25 patients (76%) were without progression and remained alive, including both patients in Cohort 1.
  • 9 (39%) patients in Cohort 2 were observed to have response according to investigator-assessed RECIST v1.1. 8 (35%) of those responses were confirmed and previously reported at ESMO 2025. 1 patient experienced an initial response in their most recent tumor assessment after the prior update at ESMO 2025. As the patient remains on study, the patient is expected to receive a subsequent tumor assessment.
  • 7 patients were observed to have deepening of best response, including 1 patient with stable disease.

Based on this, it does seem as though VMT-α-NET may have a decent shot at approval, which explains why stock climbed as high as >$5.5 per share in early February. Although we don't have PFS data yet, which is likely to be key, Perspective has noted the 13% objective response rate ("ORR") associated with current standard of care Lutathera, and the CEO has noted that:

we keep adding more sites into our clinical trial program with the hopes that if we get a clearance to a registrational trial, then we'll be able to go very, very quickly from an operational basis.

More Data Shared, More To Come

On April 20th, Perspective shared more data, based on an additional 12 weeks of follow-up since the January update. An overview of the study design is shared below:

SSTR2 is a protein that is overexpressed in neuroendocrine tumors (NETs) and other cancers. Perspective states in its 2025 10K filing that:

The median overall survival rate also varies widely in the highly heterogeneous NET populations and is based on site, stage and grade of disease. It is estimated that 80% of NETs over-express SSTR2. For this reason, somatostatin analogues are a cornerstone of the treatment of most NETs. In addition to SSTR2 analogs, low-grade and/or localized disease is amenable to surgical intervention and carries a good prognosis in terms of five-year overall survival rate (>90%), but there remains recurrence risk.

The latest data was summarised in a press release as follows:

  • Updated efficacy analysis in the same 25 patients from ASCO-GI 2026 and ESMO 2025 was presented with an additional ~12 weeks and ~25 weeks of follow-up, respectively.
  • 18 of the 25 patients (72%) were without progression and remained alive, including both patients in Cohort 1.
  • Ten (43%) patients in Cohort 2 were observed to have response according to investigator-assessed RECIST v1.1. Nine of those responses were previously reported at ASCO-GI 2026, including one initial response reported at ASCO-GI 2026 that has since been confirmed. Since then, one more patient experienced an initial response in their most recent tumor assessment. As the patient remains on study, the patient is expected to receive a subsequent tumor assessment.
  • Eight (50%) of the 16 patients in Cohort 2 whose tumors all express SSTR2 were observed to have response according to investigator-assessed RECIST v1.1.
  • Nine patients were observed to have deepening of best response since initial tumor assessments on these patients were reported at ESMO in October 2025.

Novartis' (NVS) radiopharmaceutical drug Lutathera is also approved to treat SSTR positive NETs, and earned ~$800m of revenues in 2025. That drug's pivotal study achieved a statistically significant improvement in progression free survival ("PFS"), although the study did not reach statistical significance in terms of overall survival. Given Perspective's current market cap of ~$510m, a successful study could therefore trigger a major share price upsurge. As the CEO explained at the JPM conference:

We haven't hit a PFS number yet because the patients are doing very, very well, and the drug has been incredibly well tolerated. We did receive a Fast Track designation. We had initially thought to go maybe as a post LUTATHERA initiative, but the FDA in conjunction with us really was encouraging us to go into the -- as an alternative in that frontline therapy after the patients have finished somatostatin sort of tolerance and stability.

Discussing safety, the CEO added:

We had no DLTs, no discontinuations due to adverse events. The data that we've shown at ESMO and ASCO GI has shown no dysphasia, no serious renal complications. And the only modest serious adverse events, none of which was related to the study medication. of note, we have not seen major dysphasia that has been seen in other treatments with either the standard of care, the LUTATHERA or some of the other investigational compounds. We have not seen that yet.

Perspective says it wants to speak to the FDA about initiating a registrational study (i.e. data from that study could be used to support an approval submission) at a 5 millicurie cohort dose, although the FDA has apparently also approved the company to "go higher still to 6 millicuries".

The radiopharmaceuticals market may reach ~$14bn in size by 2032, research has suggested. Through a partnership with Lantheus (LNTH) Perspective has acquired a manufacturing facility for production of its 203Pb- and 212Pb-labeled radiopharmaceuticals, and is also evaluating candidate VMT01 in combo with Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) immune checkpoint inhibitor Opdivo (nivolumab) in patients with MC1R-positive metastatic melanoma, and as a monotherapy.

Analysis - Multiple Causes For Optimism, Against Inevitable Risk Factors

In front of Perspective in 2026 then is the possibility of ongoing positive study data in NETs setting up a potential pivotal study, plus readouts from the melanoma candidate - in combo with Bristol Myers Squibb's (BMY) checkpoint inhibitor Opdivo (nivolumab), and as a monotherapy, and from the solid tumor targeting PSV359, due in late 2026.

I am therefore somewhat bullish on the investment opportunity for several reasons. Firstly, the opportunity to potentially show the lead candidate, VMT-α-NET, may have a superior safety and efficacy profile to Novartis' ~$800m per annum selling Lutathera, in NETS.

Secondly, the versatility of Perspective's approach potentially allowing the company to expand into multiple solid tumor indications. As we can see below, management believes it targets a total addressable market ("TAM") upwards of $30bn in size:

Thirdly, my belief that radiopharmaceuticals is an industry with high barriers to entry, meaning Perspective's competition is somewhat limited. The company states in its 2025 annual report / 10K filing:

There are also several companies developing alpha-based radiopharmaceuticals for the treatment of cancer, including Bayer, Novartis (NVS), Bristol Myers Squibb (through its acquisition of RayzeBio), Eli Lilly (LLY) (through its acquisition of POINT Biopharma), Sanofi (SNY) Lantheus (through its acquisition of Evergreen), Telix Pharmaceuticals (TLX), Actinium Pharmaceuticals (ATNM), RadioMedix, AdvanCell, Orano Med, Aktis Oncology, AstraZeneca (AZN) (through its acquisition of Fusion Pharmaceuticals), Convergent Therapeutics, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), ARTBIO and Abdera.

Granted, it is not a short list, however I would note the recent acquisitions - BMY paid >$4bn to acquire RayzeBio, Eli Lilly ~$1.4bn to acquire Point Biopharma, and AstraZeneca ~$2.4bn to acquire fusion. Perspective's market cap at the time of writing is almost exactly $500m, hence, I wonder if positive data shared later this year from one or two of its studies might put the company in the "shop window", with a price tag of ~$1bn, or higher?

I'd also note that, given several of the above mentioned smaller companies are not listed, Perspective represents one of only a few investment opportunities for anyone looking to gain exposure to this sector. Another candidate to consider in this field is Monopar Therapeutics (MNPR) - stock is up >35% since my Buy call last September. Telix Pharma, with its market cap of $3.6bn, is a far more expensive option, although it has a brain and a prostate cancer therapy in Phase 3 studies already.

As of the end of 2025, Perspective reported:

Cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of approximately $145M as of December 31, 2025, together with subsequent equity offering (net proceeds of ~$164 million), expected to be sufficient to fund current planned clinical milestones and operational investments into late 2027.

Net loss for the year ended December 31, 2025 was $103.1 million, or $1.40 per basic and diluted share, compared to a net loss of $79.3 million, or $1.23 per basic and diluted share, for the same period in 2024.

Cash flow from operations in 2025 was $(82.5m), so I would broadly agree management's view that funds are in place to the end of 2027, although funding a registrational study and advancing its other candidates into later stage studies will inevitably place a strain on finances, making upcoming data catalysts even more critical. Positive data could lead to a share price spike, allowing the company to potentially raise funds at a more competitive price, meaning existing investors don't suffer too much from dilution.

We should also note Perspective's clinical trials are structured in quite complex ways, and data still immature (does finding is still ongoing), so the risk that final data does not support earlier optimism is tangible, which could lead to market sell-offs - as recently as mid-January, shares traded at half the value they do today.

Nevertheless, Perspective cannot do much more than suggest with credible data in a decent number of patients that it may be superior to Novartis' Lutathera in treating neuroendocrine tumors, which it has done, and if the final data does support the early data, it's an exciting prospects for patients and for Perspective's shareholders. Meanwhile, two other candidates can stake a claim for registrational studies this year also, which hedges the risk of VMT-α-NET data disappointing.

I view Perspective as a well run, operationally strong company, with multiple candidates, some promising data slightly derisking upcoming readouts, operating in an industry with high barriers to entry, and an attractive target for Big Pharma. Hence, Perspective receives a Buy rating from me today.

data.title: Hamborner REIT: Don't Be Fooled By A High Yield - It Is Not Sustainable
data.description:
Investment Thesis Overview

Hamborner REIT’s (“HAB”) dividend yield is around ~8%, and REIT status guarantees 90% of net income to be returned to shareholders via dividend. However, in my view, HAB’s dividend is not safe at current levels and will come under pressure in the coming years as costs increase across personnel, financing, and maintenance. I estimate a total of ~12m in cost increases until FY-29, with the majority coming from increased interest costs. However, for the years FY-28e and FY-29e, I do not assume any increase in maintenance or personnel costs or any negative impact from a reduction of income from rent or leases. The cost increases will put pressure on FFO I and therefore on the dividend payments going forward. Even though I assume a yield of 6.1% in FY-29e, I also estimate a required yield of ~8% from investors. This would conclude in a four-year CAGR of just ~2.5%. The assumptions do not even take into account a potential increase in vacancy rates or property value decline. Both are possible and could further impact HAB’s financials. It is also possible that capex on the properties will increase with aging properties, reducing cash available for debt repayment and further pressuring margins. Management intends to sell down office assets in the coming years, but this could prove difficult in a weak transaction market for office properties. Even though management and the company staff do a good job, the business operations are in a tough spot. I would sell here and hunt for yield somewhere else.

Business Overview

(All figures in EUR and as of Q4 25, unless otherwise indicated)

Hamborner REIT (“HAB”) is a German-based real estate investment trust that invests in commercial real estate. The portfolio is comprised of a total of 64 properties split into retail (38 properties / ~60%) and office (26 properties / ~40%). The total fair value of the properties is at 1.35bn, with 604.8k square meters of leased area and an annualized rent of 88.4m. Yield of the portfolio is at 6.6% (based on annualized rent) and a vacancy rate of 3.5%. Tenant structure is relatively diversified except for ~34% of annualized rent generated from food retail. Around half of it is coming from EDEKA, which is a food retailer in Germany (see two charts below).

In regard to like-for-like y-o-y changes, rents grew by +0.5%, with vacancy increasing by 110 bps. The figures exclude acquisition and disposal impact, as HAB sold two assets in FY 2025 for a total of 27m.

In regard to profitability, HAB’s net rental income decreased by -1.6% yoy, with FFO FY 25 declining by -5.7% to 48.6m. Decrease of FFO was mainly impacted by higher personnel expenses, lower net rental income, and higher other operating expenses.

For FY-26, HAB expects income from rents and leases of between 87.5 and 89.5m with FFO between 38 - 42m and being the basis for Dividend payouts. Therefore, for the dividend FY-25, management proposes 0.39 DPS to the AGM (taking place on 7th May, 2026), which is in line with management’s corridor of 60 - 70% of FFO pay-out ratio.

EPRA NAV per share is at 9.07 / -7.3% yoy and implies a discount of ~47% to HAB’s share price of 4.85 (as of 25/04/26). Loan-to-value of the portfolio is at 44.3% and increased by 60 bps yoy. Total debt level is at 640m with an average cost of debt at 2.1% and net debt/EBITDA of 9.8x. The average maturity of debt is 3.2 years. The debt book consists of bank loans.

Attractive Valuation or Meager Return?

While HAB’s NAV discount of ~47% and dividend yield of ~8% might propose an attractive investment idea, it is not, in my opinion. Higher costs across income statement lines will put pressure on margins and consequently lower dividend payouts. Furthermore, it is not certain that the portfolio will hold its fair values as of FY-25. This could put additional pressure on NAV and, at the same time, increase LTV levels of the portfolio.

HAB’s historic dividend yield and estimate of required yield:

The current dividend proposed to the AGM 2026 for the financial year 2025 is EUR 0.39, implying an 8% dividend yield on a share price of 4.85 (as of 25/04/26).

Past dividend yields ranged between 4.4% and 8.6%, with higher levels since FY-22, when interest rates increased. As you can see below, the 10-year bond yield is around +300 bps higher compared to the pre-FY-22 time, when abnormally low interest rates drove real estate markets in Europe (and elsewhere). Since then, the average dividend yield increased to around 700 bps and within a range of 5.48% to 7.7% during FY23-FY25.

Based on the increased 10-year bond yield, the HAB dividend yield should also be priced at higher yields. Before higher interest rates kicked in with FY-22, the dividend yield above the 10-year yield was on average at 500 bps during FY-17 to FY-21. Since FY-22, that has compressed somewhat to 450 bps. At 280 bps 10-year bond yield, the implied dividend yield should range around 730 bps. Therefore, an ~8% dividend yield seems attractive, but HAB’s dividend at 0.39 per share is not sustainable as costs are expected to increase and HAB plans to dispose of a large share of its office property portfolio. While the latter will decrease top-line, the former will put pressure on margins and therefore lower profitability.

Cost increases will put pressure on margins:

Cost increases come at all expense lines: maintenance expenses, personnel cost as well as financing cost.

Maintenance: Already in Q4 24 when HAB guided for FY-25 figures, HAB mentioned higher maintenance to range between EUR 2-4m pa. This is an increase of at least 300% at the lower end of 2m based on FY-24 figures. The increase in maintenance is entirely earmarked for decarbonization measures. At the same time, there is an increase in capex, which will reduce AFFO. However, the dividend basis is FFO, so capex does not impact future dividend levels.

Personnel cost: Already in FY-25, personnel cost increased by ~1m, and HAB expects further increases from personnel expenses as hiring increased during H1 26. This was also mentioned during the conference call and somewhat outlined in the Q4-25 earnings presentation.

Conference call Q4-25: “Further effects on operating results are expected from the expansion of personnel capacities and the filling of vacant positions during the last month and in the first half of 2026.”

Financing cost: Lastly, the biggest driver of higher costs is refinancing mortgage-backed loans. In FY-26, a total of 16% of financial liabilities with an average of 1.3% will have to be refinanced at higher rates. I estimate around 400 bps of refinancing cost, which should lead to a total of 2.75m. However, the actual cost increase in FY-26 will be lower as the majority of mortgage-backed loans are to be refinanced in H2 26. Therefore, the full effect will only take effect in FY-27e. At the same time, there is a similar situation in FY-25 as the majority of mortgage-backed loans matured in Q4 25.

Counting in cash generated that is not paid out as dividends, HAB’s interest cost impact should total EUR -7m over the period. This assumes debt reduced by >10% / 68m to EUR 572m in FY-29e from 640m in FY-25. Debt cost should increase from -13.3m to -20.2m in the same period even though gross debt is reduced. The underlying assumption for financing cost increases is a refinancing rate of 400 bps.

Overall, I expect the dividend to decrease steadily over the coming years. This is primarily driven by the cost increases. My assumptions are shown in the chart below. The biggest impact on cost is coming from the increase of interest expense in the coming years.

It is important to note that the assumptions do not include a potential decrease of top-line either via disposals, vacancy increase, or releasing of assets at lower rates. Such a case could add further pressure on margins and, in turn, on the dividend basis (FFO I). For example, the vacancy rate in the portfolio has increased from 270 bps in FY-23 to 350 bps in FY-25. The increase is entirely coming from the office portfolio, where vacancy rates have increased from 430 bps to 580 bps. Overall, rents increased by 0.5% yoy in FY-25, impacted by positive development of indexation (+2.2%) as well as a negative impact from re-lettings and vacancy increase (-1.7%). Disposals not counting in the like-for-like figures did further reduce rental top-line by -3.1%. Therefore, a stable top line may be a conservative assumption, and it is likely that rental revenues do at least stagnate.

The full income statement forecast is shown here:

Taking everything into account and assuming a required dividend yield of 7.3%, HAB’s total return for the next three years is estimated at 4.3%. Reasons for this are the cost increases that lead to a dividend decline from 0.39 DPS25 to 0.30 DPS28e. However, in case the required dividend yield decreases and/or the interest cost decreases, the 4-year CAGR increases. The scenarios are showcased in the table below.

Clearly, 4.3% CAGR is not enough, especially considering additional risk from top-line decline and/or the decline of portfolio values. Therefore, HAB is a dividend trap that will return subdued CAGR in the upcoming years, and I recommend to sell shares and hunt for yield somewhere else.

Portfolio values as another potential downside factor:

Apart from a dividend that may decrease going forward, the portfolio may also come under pressure. Currently, the portfolio incorporates a 6.6% annualized rental yield with a value of 1.35bn and annualized rent of 88.4m. In FY-25, the portfolio of retail properties was devalued by -4.9% yoy, and office properties declined by -4.2% yoy (see chart below). Consequently, HAB shareholders lost a combined 260m or 3.20 per share, between FY-23 and FY-25. EPRA NTA lost 24% since YE-22. Last transactions show a diverse picture with disposals at a premium to book values as well as slight discounts. In case reletting rent roll is lower than assumed by the appraisers, there will be further portfolio value depreciation.

Risk to the Investment Thesis

Upside risk from lower interest rates:

The highest upside risk to the hypothesis of rising cost is a lower interest rate environment. In case of lower rates of around 250 bps, market participants should require a lower dividend yield, and total shareholder returns measured by dividend yields could be attractive. In this case, there are more interesting investment alternatives to HAB. For example, the listed German residential real estate companies may be quicker to close NAV discounts, and with a rather liquid real estate category, the direct transactions market of the residential real estate market should pick up. Furthermore, in Germany's city regions there is a strong demand overhang for apartments even though the development of residential units is at a multi-year low. It makes the case for residential companies even stronger. The data table above shows the scenario analysis of lower interest rates and lower yield requirements by market participants.

Conclusion

In my view, a 4.3% CAGR over the next years is not enough, and there are plenty of more attractive alternatives. Investors that are able to short sell the shares in a pair trade with a company are better positioned for the next years. Especially, HAB's office properties may come under further pressure in case AI leads to less demand. This, however, is not reflected in the assumptions.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Celestica Earnings: Why I'm Finally Buying In (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Celestica Inc. (CLS) shares have been flying high the last few weeks as the market seems to have noticed the existence of a growing bottleneck in network connectivity equipment, which is vital to the AI data center build-out. In this context, the company reported Q1 earnings on Monday after the close that investors seem to have deemed lackluster. Regardless, I think CLS is a solid play for the next era of AI infrastructure and will be upgrading to a full position on this dip.

In my last piece on Celestica back in January, I discussed a news report that Google (GOOG) was looking to widen its supplier base for TPUs, and I argued that this was actually a positive development, indicating the resulting selloff in CLS was overdone. Shares have since appreciated by more than 43% relative to +3.6% for the S&P 500 (SPY). That article can be read here.

Much of these gains have come in the last few weeks as the entire AI network connectivity sector has absolutely taken off:

The stocks of industry mainstays like Broadcom (AVGO) and Marvell (MRVL) have risen substantially, and Credo (CRDO), my top stock pick of 2026, nearly doubled in just a month (you can read about my CRDO thesis here).

AI stocks in general have been seeing a resurgence since the détente in the Middle East between the U.S. and Iran, but networking has been dramatically leading the pack as data center build-outs appear to be encountering an issue—there's a supply shortage of the equipment that lets GPUs talk to each other and to the Internet.

So is this legit, or are investors overestimating the windfall here? That's the trillion-dollar question.

The market certainly seems to think that the bottleneck is real and that margins are about to get a boost, which sets up Celestica's Q1 2026 earnings as a reckoning of expectations vs. reality.

Let's quickly recap the report:

As expected, Celestica's growth remained strong, with revenue coming in at $4.05 billion (+53% YoY/11+% QoQ), operating margin up 90 bps to 8%, and EPS a record $2.16 (+80% YoY/+14.2% QoQ). What you see here, dear reader, is a beautiful example of operating leverage—revenue and margins expanding in tandem. Because of burgeoning AI demand and capacity constraints, Celestica has been able to sell its products immediately and to the highest bidder.

And even though it is investing in more capex, Celestica is basically working at 100% capacity building switches and servers that are key cogs in the AI machine. This means, as it expands its manufacturing footprint slowly but surely, the company will be able to continue taking advantage of the supply-constrained environment to record margins and profits.

In that vein, Celestica raised its FY2026 guidance from $17 billion in revenue to $19 billion, from $8.75 EPS to $10.15, and from a 7.8% operating margin to 8.1%. It also provided the following outlook for Q2 2026:

These numbers tell the same story as the annual outlook and seem to validate the market's upbeat sentiment about the sector over the past month. However, the stock is down 9% after hours as I write this, which I would attribute to profit-taking considering the beat-and-raise nature of the report and the stock's 40% run over the past few weeks.

As I have covered in my previous networking stock coverage, I believe we are in the midst of what is probably the most capex-intensive secular trend in the history of the world. There are many who doubt whether AI will live up to the hype—I'm one of them—but one thing that is certainly not hype is the money hyperscalers are spending to build the compute base for the future of AI.

The top hyperscalers will spend in excess of $600 billion in 2026 to build this capacity, and that number isn't likely to go down in 2027. That means Celestica will be selling more TPU servers to Google, more Helios racks for AMD (AMD), more networking switches running on Broadcom silicon, and the list goes on. Celestica is investing in more fabs, but I see demand outstripping supply in this market for the next few years at least, and all the while, the company will be raking in high-margin revenue from hyperscalers with deep pockets.

Because Celestica is an original design manufacturer ("ODM") and not a designer of silicon, its growth will likely be more modest than some of its peers. But what it lacks in explosive growth, it makes up for with lower risk and a manufacturing moat that will be hard to cross. Because of this, CLS offers a unique value proposition in a high-beta AI portfolio, and I have upgraded my small position in the stock to a full position to capture that value.

I anticipate volatility in the short term due to the sector's run-up and the MAG7 earnings expected this week, but I expect to hold CLS for at least 5 years for the reasons stated above and will likely add on any significant dips that may arise. As such, I am upgrading CLS to a Strong Buy.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: Edison International: I Remain Careful Following My 2024 Thesis
data.description:
I write about quite a few utilities and have done so for years. When it comes to most of them that I consider attractive, I've done very well for the past 4 years - going in when cheap, then leaving when expensive, with prime examples not only Enel (ENLAY) but other European and NA-based utilities as well. Edison International (EIX), however, has not been a company that I have been favorable towards - and with good reason. When I last covered the company back towards the end of 2024, about a year and a few months ago, I covered it at about $82/share, a price that the company still has not yet recovered to. A short time after my article, as I feared, the company's share price dropped significantly to a level that meant that even a slight recovery has only put that negative RoR at close to double digits, inclusive of dividends, see below.

My thesis, at the time of writing it, was therefore entirely correct. You can find that article here.

My purpose in this article is to provide an update for EIX. The company, certainly cheaper now, has seen a relatively quick upside from what I expect is the same set of trends that we've seen in other utilities. I have cited these as being overvalued, and the upsides they have seen to be "hollow" in terms of actual fundamental appeal.

My purpose is therefore also to provide insight into whether this recent upside is as hollow or equally unattractive as other upsides in the space.

Edison is not an unattractive company per se, but it does have a few challenges worth mentioning that impact the company's appeal. First of all, most utilities that I cover are BBB or above, usually a nice BBB+. Edison is BBB-. This is not exactly a massive problem until you start looking at the coming few years. More on that below. The company's dividend yield is quite nice - we have over 5%, which is both above the average as well as above the risk-free rate. We also don't have quite the massive overvaluation that we have in the rest of the space.

But in this case, I believe there are clear reasons for this.

Because the company is set to release its 1Q26 results tomorrow, Tuesday the 28th of April, this also serves as an earnings preview for the company.

So let's look at what we have here.

Edison International - Utility Below 11x P/E, but With Some Challenging Fundamentals

Much of the utility space is currently enjoying significant forecast upsides owing to the AI craze. The combination of high infrastructure investment upside, together with data centers, together with underlying trends spells out, for bullish investors, a positive case in utility AEPS growth.

However, as I have previously made clear in other articles, what I believe these bullish investors are missing is that the CapEx programs that accompany such investments and plans from most utilities amount to either the total market capitalizations of the companies involved or close to it. That means in a period of 3-5 years, these companies are expecting to spend as much in capital expenditure for new energy demand and infrastructure as their entire business is valued on the market. This is a highly risky investment prospect, and no matter how much underlying positive AI sentiment there is, I cannot work with this assumption at a high multiple.

The CapEx in the USA alone is expected to be at about $1.4T until 2030. Any face-value estimation of this investment budget is subject to missing out on execution hurdles, regulatory risks, rate hikes, rate base inclusion risks, overbuilding risks, demand based entirely on estimation, which risks resulting in stranded assets, et al. Utilities have already increased their bill costs by about 30-40% in less than 5 years, one of the highest increases in history. I find the notion that regulators will allow these utilities to increase it even close to as much to be based on speculation. And it's not only that. Regulators could further allow project recovery for CapEx that they determine to be too contextually expensive, leading to cuts in ROE, which would cut utility financial metrics, despite the fact that the utility markets these pushes as "necessary."

The problem is that utilities are typically viewed as a good income, safe-haven sort of investment with little risk, little growth, and stability. This trend has fundamentally changed the risk factors involved in these investments, and I believe neither markets nor most investors have caught up to see this. The latest report was given roughly 2 months ago, and while the company was able to cite in-line or exceeded targets for most of its estimates, it hides a more relevant underlying truth.

I will, for the sake of argument, even ignore the Eaton fire impacts (despite those being potentially upwards of $10B-$13B) - I view these, while relevant in context, as not being that relevant to the long-term appeal of EIX. It will cost the company - current claims offers are at more than $150M, and costs may spike further.

EIX's CapEx plan is even more ambitious than many of its peers. Because EIX is valued at about $26B in market cap, its nearly $41B CapEx plan marks a record in relation to its market cap in all utilities that I have covered as of late.

If you further take into consideration that the company is already BBB- rated, this additional debt/funding added to the company will make its future appeal materially dependent on the successful execution of this plan. Because I view the likelihood of solid execution and hitting its targets as unlikely (not due to lack of appeal, more to lack of clarity in the forecast), I view this company as being one of the riskiest in the space. I view the 7% rate base CAGR growth until 2030 as insufficient to deliver appeal to this company. Current AEPS estimates for the company are negative for the year and positive for the years following, but totaling only 2-3% AEPS growth annually. This is lower than the sector, and it's even with all of the growth estimated here.

It's my stance that the market is failing to take into account the potential elevated cost of capital. The cost of debt will put further pressure on margins, and EIX is already high on debt there. Even with low debt usage, the significant dilution that using equity would mean would make the company materially less attractive. This, coupled with the increased cost of building infrastructure (building a gas-fired asset is now 2.5x as high as a few years back), means that the company may have miscalculated as well, and the cost is higher.

These underlying worries are "bubbling away" right now. Very few investors seem to be aware of them, and very few analysts who should be covering or highlighting them are, in my view, not giving them the "article space" they deserve or should have. So that's what I am doing here.

The company's massive exposure to Cali wildfires, regulatory issues, debt levels, high dividend level, materially possible dilution, and CapEx planning means that I view Edison as fundamentally unattractive except at the cheapest of prices. The company has over $36B of debt at this time alone. That exceeds its current market capitalization by a significant margin, and it's before the company has even properly started its CapEx expansion.

Despite what can only be described as healthy earnings, the company has cash flow issues. Its FCF is often negative, which is a going concern for dividend sustainability, especially in light of the massive expansions and liabilities. Interest coverage is below 3x, which is rare for a utility. Despite this, the company keeps increasing its dividend, latest at about 6% 4 months ago.

All of these things mean that, despite operationally solid performance, I consider the company to be unattractive unless you buy it very, very cheaply.

The 1Q26 is being released tomorrow (Tuesday). The estimates for the company are about $1.7/share, reflecting (if performed like this) a strong operational performance, with positive trends regarding wildfire recovery. However, my view is that this set of earnings, even if it were to materialize exactly as expected, does not change the company's overall thesis. It would still annualize to the forecasted decline for the year, and it doesn't address the issues that I see for the company in its valuation here. A positive surprise in earnings may edge things up a little - push the AEPS growth to 3% per year instead of 2.5%, but it wouldn't change the overarching risks that I see for the business.

Valuation for Edison International - Tricky, but I View It as Unattractive

The company is down 16% since my last article, and at the deepest was down close to 40%. Some might say that this proves recovery - I disagree.

Based on the company's negative AEPS estimates in 2026, followed by uncertain rates of growth at less than 3-4% per year on average, I consider this company unattractive. Because the yield to me is actually not that safe (FCF goes negative often), I wouldn't give the company extra points for this either. The company has exposure to one of the trickiest regulatory geographies in all of the USA, and this is not even to mention that California is subject to migration patterns that are unfavorable at this time.

All of this means that I consider the analyst forecasts for the company, meaning no growth or negative growth, to be accurate. And a low-growing, high-debt, uncertain-future utility with over $36B in debt and about to take on a $40B CapEx run cannot be viewed as positively as some analysts are seemingly arguing that it should. I last gave the company $74/share as a PT. That would mean that if you bought in line with my expectation, you'd have actually made money here. I did not buy in line with this because I invested in better companies like Enel.

And I'm cutting this PT for all of the reasons given above. The lowest current analyst target for EIX is $62/share. It starts approaching what I believe to be the right idea, but it's not quite there. Its CapEx plan is much higher than I expected, and the Eaton fire risk has evolved to something bigger than expected. Despite the average for analysts being at $74/share, like my PT, the vast majority of the 14 analysts, at 10, consider the company to be a "HOLD" or a "SELL."

I forecast the company using a 10x forward P/E for the year 2028, while also accounting for higher interest rates and the company not being able to get the ROE it wants due to regulatory actions. This suggests a PT of $55/share for EIX. While I don't have any solid foundations for it beyond the negative FCF, which has now been negative for 10 years, I don't consider the dividend to be necessarily sustainable in a scenario where the company sees further pressure. Even if not now, it might become unsustainable later. I therefore discount on the basis of this as well.

I believe covering this company without correctly accounting for and highlighting these risks is doing investors a massive disservice. I believe this mix of reasons is the grounds for the "HOLD" rating shared by both Wall Street and Quant averages, with Quant risks pointing to things like growth issues and revisions. I think there are lingering risks to profitability and momentum, which are currently not being correctly estimated.

For this, I say that the company warrants no more than a "HOLD" in 2026, and I want a higher discount before buying.

In my IG, Wolf of Value, I cover other utilities similar to EIX that I consider to be attractive, as well as other interesting investments for both conservative, income-oriented investors, as well as more growth-oriented investors. I also provide more succinct and clear targets and valuations, alongside personal commentary.

The thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Edison International is a utility that owns one of the most hardened sets of infrastructure assets in the California/West Coast geography in the US, and this is its primary advantage even in 2026. It's a BBB-rated business with a dividend yield of over 5%. However, the company has significant risk factors related to its CapEx plan, its fundamentals, and its regulatory environment.

Based on this conservative estimate and valuation, I consider EIX to be a “Hold.” I lower my PT to $55/share due to the increased risk that I see and demand a significant discount prior to investing.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a valuation target of $55/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.
